scenario: Granite Bank runs a Camunda 8.8 Java client embedded in a payment service that processes 3500 transactions per hour. The team wants every Java client log message to print to the console using the documented Camunda Java client logging configuration, so on-call engineers can tail standard output during incidents in the production cluster.
question: Which file must a Camunda 8.8 Java client team add to the classpath to configure logging as described in the docs?

options.text: Add log4j2.xml to the classpath of the application; it defines a Console appender with a PatternLayout and a Root logger configured at level info to print every log message
Add logback.xml to the classpath of the application; it defines a Console appender with a PatternLayout and a Root logger configured at level info to print every log message
Add log4j.properties to the classpath of the application; it defines a Console appender with a PatternLayout and a Root logger configured at level info to print every log message
Add zeebe-logging.yaml to the classpath of the application; it defines a Console appender with a PatternLayout and a Root logger configured at level info to print every log message

a.text: Correct. The Java client logging docs instruct you to add a log4j2.xml file to the classpath, declaring a Console appender that writes to SYSTEM_OUT with a PatternLayout and a Root logger at level info so every client message is logged.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented configuration uses Log4j 2 via log4j2.xml; logback.xml is for the Logback framework and is not the configuration file the Java client guide describes.

c.text: Incorrect. The Java client docs reference the Log4j 2 XML configuration (log4j2.xml), not the legacy log4j.properties file used by Log4j 1.

d.text: Incorrect. zeebe-logging.yaml is not a documented Camunda Java client logging file; the docs configure Log4j 2 via log4j2.xml on the classpath.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 Java client logging guide configures Log4j 2 by placing a log4j2.xml file on the classpath. The example defines a Console appender targeting SYSTEM_OUT with a timestamped PatternLayout and a Root logger at level info, which prints every Java client log line to the console.
